mappings.rationaleAI: Directly covers the art of theatre, theatrical production, staging, acting, directing, and theatre history -- the core subject of this entire section (A-D).
Traces the evolution of modern theatrical production through naturalism, realism, and symbolism, while exploring the audience-performance relationship (A.4, B.1.a, D.1).
Directly covers tragedy as a traditional dramatic form in theatrical production (B.1.a.i).
Directly covers comedy as a traditional dramatic form, including pantomime and Restoration comedy (B.1.a.i, B.1.a.iii).
Examines ancient Greek tragic theatre and Sophoclean stagecraft -- from the Oedipus plays to Ajax -- foundational to the origins of Western theatre (A.1, D.1).
Shakespeare is central to Western theatre as playwright, covering comedy, tragedy, and tragicomedy in performance (B.1.a.i, B.1.d, D.1).
Explores Shakespearean comedy in performance -- from early farce through romantic comedies to the late romances -- illuminating comedy as a theatrical genre and repertory staging (B.1.a.i, B.1.c).
Analyzes the major Shakespearean tragedies in performance, from Hamlet and Othello to King Lear, illuminating tragedy as a theatrical genre (B.1.a.i).
Shaw is a major modern playwright, relevant to dramatist-controlled productions and Western theatre history (B.1.d, D.1).
Investigates comedy, farce, music hall, variety, and burlesque as forms of popular theatrical entertainment (B.1.a.i, B.1.f.iii).
Explores the deep connection between ritual performance and the origins of theatre, including religious ceremonies and rites of passage (A.1, A.2, B.1.b).
Addresses architectural design principles essential to theatre buildings, stages, and auditoriums (C.1).
Introduces design principles that underpin staging, set design, and lighting in theatrical production (C.2).
Examines costume and fashion design, directly applicable to costumes as an element of theatrical production (C.2).
Explores aesthetic experience including opera and live performance, shedding light on theatre as art and audience engagement (A.1, A.4, B.1.e).
Surveys ancient Greek and Roman drama -- the tragedies of Aeschylus, Sophocles, and Euripides and the comedies of Aristophanes and Plautus -- foundational to Western theatre history (D.1).
Illuminates Indian theatrical and cinematic traditions, including theatre's evolving relationship to radio and television (B.1.h).
Examines the creative process as it applies to directing, acting, and artistic expression in theatrical production (A.6, A.7).
